summary: Martha Nussbaum discusses the capabilities approach to welfare, contrasting it with GDP and utilitarian metrics by focusing on what beings are actually able to do and be. She extends this framework to animals, arguing that justice requires protecting the characteristic strivings of all sentient creatures rather than judging them by how human-like they are.
themes: Critique of GDP as a welfare metric

principles.statement: Justice requires securing a minimal threshold of distinct capabilities, which cannot be traded off against one another.
principles.rationale: Different capabilities (like health, education, and freedom of speech) vary independently and are non-negotiably important. A society cannot compensate for pushing people below a basic threshold in one area by providing an excess in another.
principles.source_quote: it's an unjust society if people are pushed beneath that threshold on any of the 10. So you can't trade them off above a certain threshold in mind.

principles.statement: Sentience is the dividing line for justice in nature.
principles.rationale: Injustice is the wrongful blocking of a being's striving. Only sentient beings—those with a subjective viewpoint and felt desires—have strivings that can be unjustly thwarted. Plants and non-sentient entities cannot be subjects of injustice.
principles.source_quote: sentience, to me, is a great dividing line in nature. Because for me, what injustice is, is the wrongful blocking of a being's striving to get what it wants.

principles.statement: Animals should be protected for their own characteristic forms of life, not based on their similarity to humans.
principles.rationale: The traditional 'ladder of nature' with humans at the top is a false construct. Animals possess amazing horizontal variety and unique abilities (like echolocation or sensing magnetic fields) that humans lack, and they deserve protection based on their own specific strivings.
principles.source_quote: we should protect animals, because each of them is what it is. And they're not like us. And they have amazing horizontal variety.

frameworks.name: The Capabilities Approach to Justice
frameworks.when_to_apply: When evaluating the minimal justice of a society or determining the welfare of humans and animals.
steps: Identify the characteristic form of life and strivings of the specific species.
Define a list of central capabilities (spaces of choice) that are non-negotiably important to that species' flourishing.
Establish a minimal threshold for each capability based on local resources and abilities.
Evaluate whether any individual is pushed beneath the threshold on any of the capabilities.
If an individual is below the threshold, recognize it as an injustice and design interventions to break the dynamic without trading off other basic capabilities.
frameworks.source_quote: The proposal was to measure not by income and wealth, not by utility, but rather by what people are actually able to do and to be—so capabilities or opportunities to do and be things that you value.

mental_models.name: Spaces of Choice (Substantive Freedom)
mental_models.description: Viewing welfare not as a forced state of action or a feeling of satisfaction, but as a protected space where a being has the opportunity to choose activities they value.
mental_models.example: Freedom of religion provides the space to practice a religion if one chooses, but does not force anyone to care about or exercise religion.

mental_models.name: Combined Capabilities
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding true capability as requiring three layers: basic capability (innate potential), internal capability (developed skill), and the environmental opportunity to actually exercise it.
mental_models.example: A person might develop an internal interest and skill in religion, but without the external political freedom to practice it, they lack the 'combined capability' to exercise their religion.

heuristics: Measure welfare by asking: 'What am I actually able to do and to be?'
Lead with the truth and do not pretend there is a ladder of nature.

signature_quotes.text: The proposal was to measure not by income and wealth, not by utility, but rather by what people are actually able to do and to be—so capabilities or opportunities to do and be things that you value.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the core premise of the capabilities approach.

signature_quotes.text: Sentience, to me, is a great dividing line in nature. Because for me, what injustice is, is the wrongful blocking of a being's striving to get what it wants.
signature_quotes.context: Defining which entities can be subjects of justice.

signature_quotes.text: We should protect animals, because each of them is what it is. And they're not like us. And they have amazing horizontal variety.
signature_quotes.context: Rejecting the anthropocentric approach to animal rights.

signature_quotes.text: The capabilities approach emphasizes the importance of choice because capabilities are spaces within which people actually exercise the choice to do something.

anti_patterns.description: Measuring a country's welfare by Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per capita.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is an average that ignores wealth distribution (hiding misery at the bottom) and only looks at wealth, ignoring other crucial factors like health, education, and freedom.

anti_patterns.description: Using utilitarianism to calculate welfare by adding up utility into a single unit.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It pretends there is a common currency (like pleasure or preference satisfaction) and assumes raising average satisfaction improves all areas of life, ignoring that capabilities like health and freedom vary independently.

anti_patterns.description: The 'So Like Us' (anthropocentric) approach to animal rights.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It relies on a false, religious 'ladder of nature' with humans at the top, only granting rights to animals that closely resemble humans (like apes or elephants) while ignoring the unique, diverse abilities of other species.

anti_patterns.description: Believing that wild animals live unfettered lives in a pristine 'wild' unaffected by humans.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: There is no true 'wild' left; every space available for animal life is human-dominated, and human activities (pollution, climate change, poaching) constantly encroach on and harm these habitats.
